time_posted: I turn 31 today. My business made $557,000 last month. But before that, I failed 7 businesses in a row:
hook: Here’s what I tried building...
full_content:
Here’s what I tried building... 
and why I'm convinced each 'fail' got me closer to a 'win'


1. I started a clothing brand with my friend Thibault Selderslagh.

👎 We barely managed to sell 15 t-shirts. We had to order minimum 400. So we ended up with 385 t-shirts... now collecting dust somewhere.

👍 But, I learned how to create websites during this process.

↳ ... a skill I used to create the V1 of my website ColdIQ.


2. I co-founded a raffle lottery in the UK 🇬🇧

👎 I invested 40,000€ of my personal savings. We only managed to sell £1,000 worth of lottery tickets.

👍 But, I learned to run ads while desperately trying to sell these tickets.

↳ ... a skill we now use to book 50+ monthly meetings via ads. And that we offer as a service to clients (with some closing > $1M as a result!)


3. I tried to become an Instagram influencer.

👎 I found a hack that grew my account to 29,000 followers. Then it stopped working, and I've lost followers ever since. I never made a dime from the platform, nor did I get offered any free brand product.

👍 But, I learned about social media & algorithms.

↳ ... a skill that got me to be a LinkedIn influencer instead (😆). But more seriously, this led to 15,000,000+ impressions & 1,000+ inbound meetings for my company.

(And we now offer it as a service too... send a DM!)


4. I ran a sports betting affiliate website.

👎 I grew it to $6,000/mo, until it dropped to 0 overnight because of regulation changes in Belgium 🇧🇪

👍 But, I learned a lot about monetizing affiliate brand deals.

↳ ... a skill that allows us to generate > 5-figure per month in passive affiliate revenue.


5 & 6. I started an MMA Betting blog AND a Crypto blog.

👎 None of them made a single $1.

👍 But, I learned SEO & Copywriting

↳ ... skills that are responsible for driving 100s of thousands of visits to ColdIQ's website.


7. I launched a Facebook Ads agency

👎 I booked 15 meetings, and didn't manage to close a single client.

👍 But, I learned cold email in the process.

↳ ... a skill that turned into a service we currently deliver for 70+ clients, amounting to > $500K in MRR.


Takeaway?
You win, or you learn.

And each failure turns into a lesson that lets you try again... better.

So keep pushing!

P.S: Want a free strategy call with us? Book it here - https://coldiq.com/

time_posted: Claude can now build advanced n8n workflows from a single prompt.
hook: Just use this Claude project setup, and it will handle 90% of the work 👇
full_content:
Just use this Claude project setup, and it will handle 90% of the work 👇

This completely changed how we build automations at our $6.5M ARR agency…

Here’s precisely how we run this:

1. Write a basic prompt describing your workflow. 

Claude (Sonnet 4.5) will read it, break it down & output an n8n JSON file that can be imported.

2. Claude takes care of the complex implementation.

The system infrastructure that transforms Claude into an n8n workflow builder.

Our actual prompts vs. the workflows Claude ended up building.

hook: But have you seen what’s being built in Europe?
full_content:
But have you seen what’s being built in Europe?

Here are the agents I’m closely watching
…most specifically to help with GTM:

1. Attio

Their agent autonomously ingests calls/emails, auto-extracts insights, populates CRM fields and triggers workflows based on pre-set conditions.

Example use-case: After a sales call, auto-updating deal-relevant information (value, prospect).


2. Apify

Their agents, or “actors,” autonomously navigate websites, bypass anti-bot measures, and extract data.

Example use-case: Extract conference attendee lists to re-engage them later.

3. Lovable

Their agent builds full web apps from natural language. It writes code, sets up databases, deploys & iterates based on feedback.

Example use-case: Building free mini-tools to capture email addresses.


4. ElevenLabs 

Their voice agent handles inbound calls 24/7, qualifies leads, books meetings & detects emotions across 32+ languages.


5. lemlist

Their agent score leads against your ICP, navigates websites for personalization & generate multichannel sequences in ~3 minutes.

Example use-case: Letting AI prepare a multi-channel outreach sequence from scratch for human review.


6. Linkup

Their search API gives other AI agents accurate, real-time web access. Not an agent per se, but an API that powers them.

Example use-case: A prospecting agent would query LinkUp to check whether a prospect has just raised funding before writing personalised outreach.


7. Dust

Custom agents that connect to Slack, Notion, Google Drive & your CRM to autonomously perform analyses across all sources.

Example use-case: Asking the agent to help you prep for a sales call, then the agent pulls CRM notes, recent Slack threads, and other helpful docs into a brief.

8. n8n

Open-source workflow builder with AI building capabilities.

Example use-case: Creating custom, agentic GTM workflows.

time_posted: How I would find my first 10 clients if I had to restart from 0:
hook: It's how I've hit > $6.5M+ ARR in 3 years 👇
full_content:
It's how I've hit > $6.5M+ ARR in 3 years 👇

1. I'd find a ‘free’ way to get my brand out there.

In the beginning: 

You have lots of time.
You have little money.

So you want to start with a time-consuming channel that doesn’t require much funds.

This could be:
→ Writing blog articles.
→ Creating LinkedIn posts.
→ Recording YouTube videos.
→ Being active in niche communities.

I tried them all.
The one that worked best for me was LinkedIn.

It’s what I recommend to all my friends in B2B to get started.


Software you can use:
- Design: Canva or Figma
- Content Ideation: Taplio or Scripe 


2. I'd direct this ‘visibility’ to a short landing page.

Your brand efforts will gather interest.

Interested visitors will check your site or your social profiles.
You want them to understand asap how you help.

The most effective way I found to do so, is to record a 5-min video sales letter, breaking down your offering.

Some people won’t buy straight away.

For them, I’d suggest creating a lead magnet to grab their email addresses
… and nurture them with valuable content over the long term.

For the ones that didn’t provide their emails, you can use visitor identification software to de-anonymize them (in the US).


Software you can use:
- VSL Hosting: Wistia
- Landing Page: Webflow or Framer
- Visitor Identification: Midbound or Instantly.ai


3. I'd reach out to warm ‘high-fit’ leads.

Many prospects will engage with your content but won’t try to schedule a call

Still, they might be interested.

In this case, I’d suggest reaching out to every prospect who’d make an ideal client but hasn’t messaged you yet.

I recommend writing your first 25-50 messages by hand, before trying to automate.

This could look like:
“Hey {{name}}, seems my post on {{topic}} resonated with you. Are you working on {{potential initiatives}}? Happy to swap notes if that’s helpful.

Whatever you write… the magic of manual notes is that it forces you to re-read & improve your messaging every time.

This allows you to uncover patterns…
Which will be invaluable once you scale your sending.


Software you can use:
- Email Outreach: Instantly.ai
- LinkedIn Outreach: Expandi
- Multichannel Outreach: lemlist
- Email Finding: Wiza, Prospeo.io, LeadMagic or FullEnrich.


4. I'd repeat the above steps for 3 months straight before considering giving up. 

This 100% works.
It sounds simple, but it’s challenging to keep working hard when things are uncertain.

Working hard is difficult on its own.
But working hard, when doubting that there’s a point, is even harder.

To make it work, you must somehow convince your brain that you’re not doing this for nothing. Even when you get 0 results in the first weeks, or months.

Repeat these steps enough times.
And you’ll inevitably improve.

Improve enough…
And soon enough, you’ll reap the rewards of your hard labour.

Good luck ✌️

P.S: Who's starting something new in 2026? 👇

time_posted: Valley’s GTM machine books more meetings than 10 SDRs with just 1 person.
hook: Zayd, their CEO, is convinced every GTM can replicate it 👇
full_content:
Zayd, their CEO, is convinced every GTM can replicate it 👇

1. Content Machine

- Zayd does bi-weekly content interviews on Riverside & turns these into shorts.
- They record all his calls using Fathom.ai / Fireflies.ai and mine them for content.
- He posts 5x per week on LinkedIn & leverages tools such as Taplio & Bluecast for inspiration.
- Their LinkedIn company page mainly comprises social proof posts (e.g, live reactions of new users discovering their product).

He takes screenshots with Shottr and turns them into good-looking graphics.

His personal account focuses on a few content pillars, namely: his founder journey & GTM industry insights.


2. Signal-Based Outbound

Every week he extracts:

- His profile viewers.
- His company page followers. 
- His post engagers (personal & company).
- His website visitors (via Valley, RB2B, & Vector 👻).

Valley autonomously qualifies & tiers each prospect (high vs. medium vs. low fit), removes competitors and enriches contacts.


3. Personalized Messaging

For every high-fit lead, Valley:

- Conducts additional research
- Writes relevant messages.
- Delivers > 30% replies.

This amounts to 40+ positive replies every day in their LinkedIn inbox.


4. Lead Orchestration

When leads reply positively, but end up ghosting, they’re pushed to Slack, with their contact details, via a Zapier automation.

Then, Alex Nelson, Valley's Founding AE, calls to check if there’s still interest.


5. Deal Closing

- They send one-pager proposals via paage.io, which tracks what part of the proposal prospects spend most of their attention on.
- Payments are automatically processed through Stripe.

This entire setup books them > 150 qualified meetings per month.

....and can be reproduced by most B2B growth leaders!

P.S: What would you advise Zayd and his team to get above 200 monthly qualified meetings?

time_posted: We completely changed the way we run GTM at ColdIQ.
hook: And now, we’re making it official 👇
full_content:
And now, we’re making it official 👇

ColdIQ started as an outbound agency.

It’s still the biggest chunk of the work we do.
It’s how I found our first clients.
And it’s still an essential part of our GTM engine.

But as we ran our own go-to-market at ColdIQ…

We started experimenting with new channels:

- Google Ads
- Meta Ads
- SEO
- YouTube
- Quora Posts
- Reddit Posts
- Niche Communities
- Newsletter Sponsorships

… but most importantly:

→ LinkedIn Content
→ LinkedIn Ads

By adding LinkedIn content to the mix, we built one of the most powerful distribution engines in the GTM niche.

30M impressions.
2500+ B2B meetings.
250,000+ followers gained.
$4,000,000+ in deals influenced.

And we got much better results with our cold emails, without changing anything, just because this content engine built so much social proof.

And then, we pushed it one step further. 

We realised we had published so many proven posts… that if we pushed these in front of more of our target personas… we’d potentially book even more meetings.

So we did…

And, once again, the numbers looked great.
(LinkedIn Ads aren't 'that' simple, but you get the gist!)

This is when I started doubting the ‘logic’ behind our offering.

We’re killing it at 3 B2B channels…
So why are we delivering just 1?

If a GTM Leader in B2B Tech came to me asking: “Hey Mich, I have $20K to spend per month on my GTM… what shall I do?”

I wouldn’t tell them: “Just send as many cold emails as humanly possible”

I’d tell them to run a GTM engine that syncs Outbound, with LinkedIn Content & LinkedIn Ads.

So we quietly started offering this to clients.
But didn’t advertise it on our site.

Up until the results were undeniable.

It’s worked so well for us.
It’s worked amazingly well for orgs like AirOps. 
(who closed >$1M via our ABM engine & went from $2M to $15M just this year)

And I’m betting it’ll work for you if you’re a GTM Tech, AI SaaS or B2B Tech company.

Here's every tool I've used to grow my audience:

1. Content Ideation

→ Taplio (go.coldiq.com/taplio)

I use their in-app feed to discover outlier LinkedIn posts in my niche.

You can enter keywords like: "ai sales", "outbound" or "GTM" and then filter for posts that:

- were published within the past month

I then reverse-engineer the structure, hook & CTAs of these top-performing posts to refine my own.

Excellent for content ideation.

It lets you aggregate posts by author, which is a useful way to spot 'viral publications' on a given topic.


2. Copywriting

→ Grammarly

As a non-native English speaker, I make plenty of spelling mistakes.

I use Grammarly's extension to fix my writing in real-time.

Doesn't sound exciting, but defo a must-have in my LinkedIn stack.

→ Claude Code

I use Opus within Claude Code to generate hook variations, repurpose past content & brainstorm fresh angles.

Since it has so much context on my business, it can fill in the blanks without me needing to feed it the same info over & over.

→ Conductor

My interface to run Claude Code (& Codex).

Makes it much nicer to write/repurpose posts thanks to their beautiful UI.


3. Visuals & Design

→ Figma / Adobe Suite

My LinkedIn strategy leans heavily on visuals.

When done well, they multiply the reach of your publications by up to 10X.

Our designers use these to create our carousels & infographics.


→ Canva

My first-ever carousels published on here were made on Canva.

It's the most beginner-friendly app to create social media visuals.


4. Video Creation

→ OpusClip

I use Opus's AI features to automatically generate short-form videos from my long-form content.

The output isn't as polished as a professional editor's...
But it's cost-effective & fast.

Several AI-edited videos got me 50K+ views on LinkedIn.

→ Screen Studio

Screen Studio is my favourite Mac screen recording app.

It's ideal for making video walkthroughs or tutorials because it automatically zooms/focuses on the parts of the screen you're actively working on.

It makes video outputs look significantly more polished than they'd be otherwise.

→ Ezgif

I use Ezgif to create .GIF files from my short audioless video content.

If you're planning to publish a < 15-second audio-less video on LinkedIn...

I'd suggest speeding it up and converting it into a GIF file.

I'm not 100% sure why, but it outperforms in terms of reach & engagement.


5. Content Planning

→ Notion

My central content hub.
It's where I gather ideas, draft them, and prepare my posts.

Notion also serves as my content calendar.


That's the full stack I used to go from 0 to 65,000+ followers.

It's also the tech stack I plan to get past 100,000.

time_posted: AI changed the SDR role in 2025.
hook: What was taking hours, now takes minutes.
full_content:
What was taking hours, now takes minutes.

Did that make SDRs any less valuable?

I actually believe the opposite…
But GTM teams need to adapt to this shift.

The Old way:

A) An SDR receives a list of target accounts.
B) They reach out via email, phone & LinkedIn.

The New way:

A) Specialists own a channel
B) Systems sync outbound across channels.


What does this mean for SDRs?

In my opinion, one of two things:

1. You double down on ‘human’ activities.

AI isn’t coming for people who are great at cold calling just yet.

In a world where you can automate everything, most will be looking for shortcuts.

This means relatively fewer people hitting the phone.
And here’s the opportunity.

Along with spending more time on:
- Working complex deals
- Relationship building & multithreading

A.k.a the things AI don’t do that well, and that your peers won’t want to do anymore.

2. You lean into the technical side of GTM

You learn how to use AI & technologies like Apify, Clay, Relevance AI, Common Room, Instantly.ai, lemlist, Cursor, Claude Code.

And you push AI & tech to its limits.

Ask yourself in which areas does AI completely outperform humans.

i.e:
- AI can detect patterns across millions of data points.
- AI can score & research 10,000 prospects in a few minutes.
- AI can run 10s of workflows at the same time without tiring itself up.


The best SDR teams have both:

→ GTM Engineers that build large scale prospecting systems & feed laser-accurate data to SDRs.

→ SDRs who book meetings via manual outreach & the phone.


That’s what we see our clients with the best results to.
That’s we implemented for $1B+ organisations.

Curious, have you seen this shift in 2025?

2️⃣ We made it as effortless as possible to post

Successful LinkedIn posts have a “recipe”.

It’s typically a combination of:
→ Well-structured, breathable writing
→ A proven angle or trendy topic
→ A strong hook
→ Nice visuals

But: 

Brainstorming hooks can be hit-or-miss.
→ I encourage my team to reuse variations of mines that outperformed.

Identifying proven topics isn’t easy.
→ We put Taplio & Scripe at their disposal to help the team find outliers.

Designing nice visuals takes time.
→ We put designers at their disposal, so they can focus on the ‘writing’ vs. needing to work on the ‘packaging’.


3️⃣ We invested resources in training the team

Making this work required 1:1 sessions in which we:

- Rewrote entire posts & hooks.
- Brainstormed post ideas

- Ran team-wide sessions sharing best practices.

We’d ask applicants whether they wanted to grow their brands.
But also, many new joiners heard from us via LinkedIn.

If WE attracted them, they were more likely interested to grow.

Kenny learned about ColdIQ from a post by Monika.

time_posted: My LinkedIn content creation process that produced 20M+ impressions in 2025:
hook: (And added $4,000,000 in net ARR this year)
full_content:
(And added $4,000,000 in net ARR this year)

1. Identify who you're selling to.

This might sound obvious, but I see this mistake all the time. 

Creators focus on content they find interesting overall.
They end up educating their competitors rather than their target buyers.

E.g: I want revenue leaders to consume my content. 
It's purposefully NOT designed for other GTM agencies.


2. Identify who you want to see your content.

Revenue leaders might be your target ICP.
But SDRs might be the ones sharing it with their manager.

It's ok for your content to cast a wider net if there's a path for it finding your target buyer.


3. Study what has been produced already.

Look at what your competitors produce.

↳ How do you make it better, easier to understand or nicer to look at?

Look at your own existing content.

↳ How can you make more of the pieces that outperformed, or variations of these?

Look at Thought-Leaders in your niche/industry.

↳ What's the gap in quality between your content & theirs? 

Ask yourself, how can you close this gap?

Are they more knowledgeable than you are... or are they packaging their content better?


4. Find proven content ideas.

Based on point #3. 
Start collecting outlier content in your niche.

Use platforms like Taplio & Scripe to find viral pieces in your niche, or adjacent niches.

Reflect on how these proven formats can be adapted to your expertise.


5. Identify content pillars & recurring formats.

To make content work for you, you'll need to produce a certain quantity.

Find 2-3 topics you'll keep talking about week over week.
Find 2-3 topic formats you'll keep using to package your content.

The key to making content work = identifying a combination of proven topic angles & format that you decline over & over.

The content doesn't need to be new all the time.
It needs to be shown to new people.


6. Alternate between TOF, MOF & BOF pieces.

Some content should bring new people into your ecosystem (TOF).
Some content should pique their interest in your offering (MOF).
Some content should get them to take action (BOF).

It's rare for a content piece to do all these 3 things at once.
Decide what the goal of your piece is.


7. Package your content with nice visuals

An image is worth a thousand words.
A video is thousands of images.

You can't go wrong adding attention-grabbing designs to your written pieces.


BONUS:
- Reply to comments (it helps the algorithms but also gives you a pulse around what your target audience is wondering)
- Plan your content (you get much better results if you know in advance what your week looks like content-wise).
- Stay consistent (most quit before they see results. Don't let that be you).
- Start conversations with 'ideal engagers' (more convos = more sales).


P.S: If you need help building a GTM system that includes high-performing B2B content, send a DM :)

time_posted: OK- this is going to sound ridiculous.
hook: But, I’ve made well over $2M from posting infographics on LinkedIn.
full_content:
But, I’ve made well over $2M from posting infographics on LinkedIn.

… and Ada Beltrán created most of these for me.

So here’s the thing:

My LinkedIn journey started with ‘text-only’ publications.
(Like everyone, I guess).

But early on, I started adding some visuals that I’d make myself.

I’d spend 3 hours creating a 7-page carousel.
For it to not even look pretty.

But crazy enough, this ugly visual was getting me 2-3x the likes & the impressions.

So I figured: “if I can write the exact same thing, but get 3x the engagement by just adding an image… why not do it every single time, right?!”

And so this was the beginning of a journey that led me, & then my team, to flood LinkedIn with infographics of all sorts.

20M+ impressions later, it looks like these visuals are still going strong.

My best explanation for why this performs so well is that ‘text-only’ posts take up very little space in the feed.

What this means is that you only have the first 2 lines of your ‘hook’ to entice people into clicking the “see more” button.

But when you publish a 1050 x 1380 image, it takes up the entire space on the feed. People can’t not see the image, and you can actually use it as a second hook by writing a big title on the image. And if it’s detailed enough, it can stand on its own as a post…

But then, everybody started publishing infographics as well.

So I did something even more ridiculous, which was:

Posting ANIMATED infographics.

So here’s the thing:

An animated infographic serves the same purpose as a static one does.

But it holds attention even more, especially if you speed it up, as it loops on itself, making it look like people are watching it over & over (in LinkedIn's eyes, I guess).

So if you want to tap into extra impressions without much more effort, have a look at the infographic below, breaking down how to create animated infographics (#infographiception).

Then, take your best-performing text post ever.
Add a nice visual to it that ‘respects’ the rules laid out below.

And I’ll bet you’ll be thanking me for how well the post performs ✌️

time_posted: How the top 1% make Cold Email work in 2026:
hook: (Based on 1,000,000+ emails analyzed via Instantly.ai)
full_content:
(Based on 1,000,000+ emails analyzed via Instantly.ai)

Here's what the best-performing campaigns had in common:


1. Small Targeted Lists > Big Broad Lists

Micro-lists of 500-1,000 hyper-targeted prospects beat blasting 100,000 contacts every time.

Reply rates: 20-30% vs. 2-3%.

→ Stop praying someone bites. Start targeting the actual people who have a reason to reply.


2. Hyperenriched Data > Basic Data

Go beyond name + email. Collect:

- LinkedIn headline & profile
- Job postings (signals growth/hiring needs)
- Technologies used
- Funding announcements
- Website case studies

→ Personalization at scale requires data at scale.


3. AI Personalization > Generic Openers

Instead of: "Hey John, hope all is well at [Company]"

Try:
- Job postings → "Saw you're hiring 3 AEs..."
- Funding news → "Congrats on the $25M Series B..."
- Case studies → "Just read your case study on..."
- Tech stack → "Noticed you recently added [tool]..."

→ Make every email feel 1:1.


4. 4-Step Sequence > 1 Single Email

Most replies come from the first emails, but follow-ups increase overall sequence reply rates significantly.

- Email 1: Personalized opener + value offer
- Email 2: Short follow-up (3-5 days later)
- Email 3: Different angle (3-5 days later)
- Email 4: Breakup email (3-5 days later)

→ Keep them short (2-4 sentences). Reference the original. Add new value.

Pro tip: Layer in LinkedIn touches between emails for omnipresence.


5. Value-First > Ask-First

Stop asking for their time immediately.

❌ "Can we hop on a call tomorrow at 2pm?"
❌ "Do you have 15 minutes to chat?"

✓ "Would it make sense to send over a quick example deck?"
✓ "Happy to share what's working best right now."
✓ "Can I send you something that could help [specific pain]?"

→ They raise their hand first. Then you've earned the conversation.


6. Fundamentals > Fancy Tactics

Master the 3 core pillars:

1. ICP – Who exactly are you targeting?
2. Offer – What specific outcome do you deliver?
3. Copy – How do you communicate value?

→ Depth > Width. No shiny object syndrome.


7. Long Game > Quick Wins

Cold email isn't a magic pill. It's a compounding client acquisition system.

→ Quality over rushing. Pipeline over quick wins. Be there when they're ready to buy.


8. Deliverability > Volume

None of this matters if you land in spam.

- Multiple domains (not just one)
- 30 emails per inbox per day max
- Proper technical setup (SPF, DKIM, DMARC)
- 30 days minimum warm-up
- Clean, validated lists

→ Sending 100 emails/hour from one email = spam city.


9. Tech Stack

- Instantly.ai (sequencing, deliverability, analytics)
- Clay (data enrichment, intent, personalization)
- Prospeo.io (list building, targeting)

Looking for more details? 👇

Check out the Cold Email cheatsheet below.

P.S: What's working for you right now with cold email?

time_posted: LinkedIn content got ColdIQ past $550,000 in MRR.
hook: And this workflow turns each of our posts into pipeline:
full_content:
And this workflow turns each of our posts into pipeline:

Here's the step-by-step breakdown:

STEP 1: CAPTURE REAL-TIME ENGAGEMENT

Each profile view, like and comment gets automatically tracked.

Someone engaging with you or your content doesn't mean they'll buy from you... But it could be a sign.

STEP 2: DATA ENRICHMENT

For each of these engagers, Clay enriches data on the person & their company.

- Job title
- Tech stack
- Hiring signals
- Company size
- Funding rounds

All of this information is enriched within minutes.

STEP 3: AI QUALIFICATION

Most of the people who engage with your content aren't a good fit for your offerings.

We use OpenAI & Claude's flagship models to score each contact.

- Great fit? We moved them to the next step.
- Not a fit? We stop the enrichment process here.

AI qualified based on criteria such as:

- B2B vs. B2C
- Geography fit
- Industry match
- Company size match
- Estimated customer LTV
- Seniority, Function & Job Title

STEP 4: CRM DEDUPLICATION

Before doing anything more, we verify: is this lead already in our records?

→ Is it an existing customer?
→ Are we already in talks with them or their team?

If so, skip them.

→ Are they a net new contact?

If so, push them to sequence.

At scale, it's difficult to avoid it entirely...
But you want to avoid reaching out to existing customers.

STEP 5: WATERFALL ENRICHMENT

We find contact data via a waterfall that includes Wiza, Prospeo.io, FullEnrich & LeadMagic.

This allows us to maximise our coverage, and get from ~50% verified email to > 85%.

STEP 6: CONTEXTUAL OUTREACH

Depending on the prospect's tier, we'll engage via email or multi-channel outreach.

Each prospect gets a tailored message via Instantly.ai or lemlist that references the topic of the post they engaged with.

Something along the lines of:

"Hey {{first_name}}, saw you engage with posts on {{topic}}, so I figured you'd be interested in this breakdown laying out how Snowflake sourced 60% of its pipeline from SDRs using {{unique mechanism}}"

STEP 7: POSITIVE REPLIES & BOOKED MEETINGS

This system consistently books 100+ qualified meetings for ColdIQ.

And it runs 24/7 on autopilot.

→ Every content piece can become a lead magnet.
→ Every engagement can turn into a qualified opportunity.
→ New conversations happen every day.

If you're posting on LinkedIn and not at least monitoring WHO engages with your posts... you're missing out.

Are you currently tracking your LinkedIn engagement?

P.S: We can build this workflow for you, alongside other GTM plays, including some similar to those we built for AirOps... that helped them get from $2M to $15M ARR just this year.

time_posted: AI agents are replacing human employees.
hook: Funny enough, they operate in very similar ways:
full_content:
Funny enough, they operate in very similar ways:

The smartest agents don’t work alone.
They work in teams.

Some behave like assistants.
Others as managers.

And as with any sort of team, there are different ways to structure their collaboration.

To illustrate this:
We can look at how 3 multi-agent setups would handle the same task…

For example: Scheduling a quarter business review with an important client.


1️⃣ Hierarchical setup

A manager agent assigning tasks to its agent team members.

1. The manager receives a task = Schedule a business review.

2. It tells a subordinate agent to check potential times to meet with the clients.

3. Another agent checks product usage from the client. "Has usage recently dropped? Is engagement still strong?"

4. A 3rd agent drafts an email based on client availability & product usage: 

"Hey Achilles, it's been a while - wanted to ensure you're using the product to the fullest. How about a quick chat next week, Wednesday?”

5. The manager reviews everything and sends the email.


2️⃣ 'Human-in-the-Loop' 

A smart assistant tapping your shoulder when needed.

1. The agent drafts the business review email and finds some available times.

2. But… it sees the client gave negative feedback 2 weeks ago.

3. Before sending the email, it asks you: “Do you want to review the email before I send it?"

4. You make small tweaks, and you send it.


3️⃣ 'Sequential' setup

Like a factory or an assembly line where each person does one task and hands it off to the next.

1. 1st Agent asks: "Is a quarterly business review due?" based on the client's last meeting time.

2. 2nd Agent pulls client's usage data.

3. 3rd Agent drafts a short email.

4. 4th Agent sends the email to book the meeting.


So, when should you use which?

As a rule of thumb:

- Hierarchical setups work well for complex, multi-step tasks.

- Human-in-the-loop setups are recommended for high-stakes, sensitive tasks.

- Sequential setups for ordered workflows with clear dependencies.


P.S: What's one process in your team that would run better...
with some smart agents working behind the scenes? 👇

time_posted: The Content Marketer role changed because of AI.
hook: Publishing 2,000+ words used to take hours…
full_content:
Publishing 2,000+ words used to take hours…
Now it takes minutes.

So does that make content writers any less valuable?

Eoin Clancy & I think otherwise.
But Content teams need to adapt to this shift.

The Old Way:

A) A Content Marketer performs keyword research on Ahrefs.
B) They write SEO optimized blog posts 1-by-1.
C) They acquire backlinks & wait for the pieces to rank.

The New Way:

A) A Content Engineer captures the best content strategies into repeatable workflows (prompts, templates, rules)
B) They blend AI and human review to scale QUALITY content production.
C) They automate distribution to gain high-leverage offsite placements.


What does that mean for Content Writers?

In my opinion, one of 2 things:

1/ You double down on ‘deep copywriting skills’.

AI isn’t coming for people who can write in-depth content pieces with a unique POV.

This means more time spent on:

- Storytelling
- Researching your topics
- Interviewing thought-leaders
- Creatively visualising knowledge

2/ You lean into the technical side of content

Learn how to use AI and technologies such as AirOps, Claude & others to build Content Systems.

The best Content teams have both:

→ Content Engineers build systems, create in-depth content creation workflows and deploy high-quality, authoritative content at scale.

→ Content Strategists anchor truth, gathering cross-functional input and preparing the knowledge foundation for workflows.

That’s what we do for ourselves.
That’s why our SEO is up, even in the age of AEO.
That’s what companies that win at AI Search do.

…and that’s why platforms like AirOps keep adding $1B+ organisations to their client portfolio.

time_posted: How I get > 30% replies via LinkedIn DMs
hook: By doing the opposite of the 100+ weekly pitches I get:
full_content:
By doing the opposite of the 100+ weekly pitches I get:

CONTEXT

I sent 5M+ outbound messages for my $6.5M+ ARR Agency.

Nowadays...

Answering DMs costs me more time than running campaigns.

I'm the CEO at a 33-person company.
(I can't imagine how much outreach execs in big orgs get)

That said, I know most execs check their inbox.
And many reply.

Here's how I decide whether I'll respond or not...

And how I changed my own outreach because of this ↓


✅  DMs offering free work

Some DMs offered a free landing page rewrite or samples of already-made work.

I ran prospecting campaigns and pitched free custom lead lists.
(These were getting me more positive replies than I could handle)

My recommendation: Imagine your prospect is an existing client. What's the first thing you'll do for them? Do it already and send it over!


✅ DMs asking to be referred

Let's say you:
1. Send a message based on a current initiative of ColdIQ
2. Searched who is in charge of this initiative.
↳ I'll gladly refer you to said person

PS: You can automate this 'play' via Clay by combining intent signals & retrieving a leader/manager in a specific department.


❌ DMs asking for time on the 1st touchpoint

Meetings are my personal hell.
I'm fighting every day to eliminate as many of them as possible.

If you're "just asking 15 min of my time" to pick my brain, I won't answer.


✅ DMs from ‘known’ people

An unfair truth in cold outreach: $WHO sends a message matters even more than WHAT the message is.

If you're a well-known executive, I'll most likely reply.

I saw this firsthand as I grew my LinkedIn to 60K+ followers...

The exact same message would perform better, without changing anything.

Pro tip: If you sell B2B products, build your brand on LinkedIn.


❌ Generic templated outreach

The only way to make impersonal, generic outreach work is to send at a massive volume (millions of messages).

LinkedIn doesn't allow such volume.

So you'd be better off doing this by email.
Or better yet... not at all.


✅ DMs sharing valuable insights

Jihad explained this better than I could under one of my posts:

"
The best "free work" isn't actually work → it's insights.

I send technical audits of prospects' platforms.
→ 5-minute analysis
→ 3 specific optimization opportunities 
→ No strings attached

Example: "Your checkout flow has a 23% drop-off at payment verification. Here's why + 2 quick fixes."

- Takes me 10 minutes.
- Shows immediate value.
- Demonstrates expertise without asking for anything.

The key: Make it so valuable they'd typically pay for it.

Generic "free consultation" = everyone does this.
Specific actionable insight = rare.
"

Solving prospects' issues before they pay you a dime is the fastest way to gain their trust.


🔘 Videos or Voice Notes

I listen to voice notes or watch videos.

It annoys people.
But if they're well done, they'll grab attention.

What's a DM you receive you could NOT ignore?

Share it 👇

time_posted: 44 data sources to find prospects in any niche...

...and 7 different ways to source this data:


1. Leverage sourcing agents

Use platforms like Airtop, Clay, Exa, Perplexity, Manus AI or Relevance AI and describe in plain/natural language what you're looking for.

Their agents handle the rest.


2. Use 'standard' B2B databases

Use filters such as “job title”, “location”, “employee count”, “headcount growth” within standard databases like Prospeo, Wiza, Apollo, LinkedIn & DiscoLike 

…or using niche databases like Openmart, influencers.club… 

to find your target data.


3. Target by technology

Use APIs like PredictLeads, or platforms like TheirStack, BuiltWith & BuyerCaddy to find companies using a specific technology.

Their use of this tech should suggest they’re a great lead for you.


4. Target companies showing buying intent for your offering category.

Monitor companies’ activity, including:
- Job openings
- Funding events
- Social engagement
- Tech/software changes

Via tools like Clay, PredictLeads, Common Room, and Trigify.io to infer potential buying willingness. 


5. Custom data extraction

Extract data from popular websites, specific pages, or any site using Apify actors or tools like Instant Data Scraper, Firecrawl, ZenRows & Airtop.


Once you sourced your target people & companies, you’ll want to:

→ Find their email addresses & phone numbers with point solutions (e.g: Prospeo, Wiza, LeadMagic) or…
